title: Lecture 11: Rift Valleys and Block Mountains

content_sections.title: Fundamentals of Rift Valleys and Block Mountains

blocks.text: In 'Internal Structure and Plate Tectonics,' we established that the Earth's crust is fragmented into rigid lithospheric plates that undergo constant motion due to mantle convection currents. This movement directly leads to crustal deformation, specifically through tensional and compressional forces, which manifests in the formation of rift valleys and block mountains.

Block mountains, or horsts, are formed when tectonic forces cause the crust to crack and fault. When tension or compression causes a block of land to be pushed upward relative to the surrounding land, a block mountain is created. These are characterized by steep sides and a relatively flat summit. The Satpura Range in India is a classic example of a block mountain located between the Narmada and Tapi rift valleys.

Rift valleys, or grabens, occur when the central block of the crust is dropped downward between two parallel fault lines. This vertical displacement occurs due to crustal extension (divergence). The floor of the rift valley is often flat and geologically distinct from the surrounding elevated plateaus. The Narmada and Tapi valleys in India are iconic examples of rift valleys formed due to tectonic subsidence.

The Peninsular Block of India, being a rigid mass, has largely remained stable since the Cambrian period; however, these internal tectonic adjustments have created distinct geomorphic features. The Narmada and Mahanadi rivers occupy these subsided fault zones. Unlike typical east-flowing rivers that form large deltas, these west-flowing rivers in rift zones often form estuaries because the rift structure prevents the accumulation of significant silt in a deltaic pattern.

This understanding becomes critical when we study 'Drainage Patterns' because the structural control exerted by rift valleys determines the orientation and flow direction of India's major west-flowing rivers, directly impacting estuarine ecosystems.

content_sections.title: Advanced Analysis — Rift Valleys and Block Mountains

blocks.text: According to Savindra Singh’s 'Physical Geography,' rift valleys are categorized into various types based on the nature of faulting: Simple Rift Valley, where the central block subsides between two parallel normal faults; and Complex Rift Valley, featuring a series of step faults. The East African Rift system is the world's largest active continental rift, representing a divergent boundary in the early stages of ocean formation.

Block mountains can be further classified as tilted or symmetrical. A tilted block mountain has one side with a steep scarp and the other with a gentle slope. This is often observed in the block-faulted topography of the Great Basin in the USA (Basin and Range Province). In the Indian context, the structural integrity of the Peninsular block is periodically stressed by intra-plate seismicity along these ancient fault lines, such as the Narmada-Son lineament.

The mechanism of formation relies on the 'horst and graben' model. Horst refers to the uplifted block, and Graben refers to the subsided block. When crustal tension exceeds the brittle strength of the rock, normal faulting occurs, leading to the formation of multiple parallel blocks. These areas are sites of intense geological activity and are often associated with high heat flow and, occasionally, volcanic activity as the crust thins.

content_sections.title: NCERT Reference — Rift Valleys and Block Mountains

blocks.text: Reference: NCERT Class 11, 'India: Physical Environment', Chapter 2 (Structure and Physiography). Key points extracted:

1. The Peninsular block is a rigid mass that has remained stable since the Cambrian period, with minor tectonic exceptions. 2. The western coast of the Peninsula has been submerged, while other parts have experienced vertical movements. 3. Block faulting is evidenced in features like the Narmada, Tapi, and Mahanadi rift valleys. 4. The Satpura Range is identified as a block mountain. 5. Malda Fault in West Bengal separates the north-eastern plateau (Karbi Anglong/Meghalaya) from the Chotanagpur plateau.

1. Confusing Rift Valleys with Deltas: Students often assume all large river valleys form deltas. However, rivers in rift valleys (Narmada/Tapi) typically form estuaries because the rift structure limits the formation of wide, silt-laden coastal plains. 2. Origin of Satpura: UPSC questions often contrast 'fold mountains' (Himalayas) with 'block mountains' (Satpura/Vindhyas). Ensure not to categorize the Satpura as a fold mountain. 3. Malda Fault confusion: Candidates often mistake the Malda fault as a result of recent plate collision; it is actually a structural gap associated with the Peninsular block's tectonic movement. This is critical for understanding the isolation of the Meghalaya plateau from the Chotanagpur plateau.
